input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building as the physical asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, characterized by the integration of static models and real-time operational data to enable simulation, analysis, and optimization across its entire lifecycle from initial design to operation and maintenance phases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real-time monitoring insights, predictive analytics for maintenance and performance forecasting, optimization of resource utilization and energy efficiency, and enhanced decision-making capabilities for stakeholders including architects, engineers, and facility managers throughout the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation utilizes Eclipse Hono and Ditto for secure, scalable real-time data exchange between IoT devices and the Digital Twin platform, alongside Unreal Engine for high-fidelity, interactive 3D visualization and immersive simulation capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include geometric representations from Building Information Modeling (BIM) enriched with Building Control System (BCS) components such as sensors, actuators, and controllers defined by Building Control Engineers. Behavioral models encapsulate control logic and device interactions. Data sources comprise enriched BIM models and real-time operational data from physical infrastructure. Data management leverages Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to automatically generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts, establishing a unified semantic foundation for continuous synchronization between physical and digital entities through real-time data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators and controllers modeled within BIM by Building Control Engineers, with control signals transmitted to the Building Control System (BCS) for physical infrastructure operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors defined within BIM by Building Control Engineers, with sensor configurations and feedback embedded into the BIM model for real-time data collection and monitoring of physical building conditions.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Validation of digital twin fidelity uses the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to quantify synchronization between digital and physical systems, measuring how well the DT reflects its physical counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, enabling continuous synchronization between physical assets and digital twins via sensor data transmission.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication enables control signals from the digital twin to the Building Control System (BCS), with system feedback confirming execution. Specific command types, message formats, and validation protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Eclipse Hono and Ditto facilitate data exchange, with the Hono/Ditto Configurator extracting metadata such as tenant ID, device ID, and endpoint addresses. Network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Model-driven framework with modular architecture and standardized extension points supports scalable deployment and runtime configuration management for dynamic adaptation. Specific hosting platforms, computational requirements, and deployment models are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle comprises initial device modeling within the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers, followed by Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to structured representations, automatic generation of deployable artifacts, real-time data synchronization between physical and digital systems, interactive visualization for monitoring and control, and modular integration of custom services to support operational evolution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, ensuring adaptability to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is provided via interactive visualization interfaces for real-time system monitoring and control, with analytics capabilities delivered through custom services integrated via standardized extension points in the modular architecture.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework standardizes BIM semantics through a model-driven transformation pipeline, creating a unified semantic foundation for Building Control System (BCS) components. It ensures compatibility with AECO-specific standards such as BIM and employs standardized extension points to enable interoperability with existing industry systems and facilitate integration of custom services.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system as a building within the AECO domain and describes its characteristics regarding the integration of static models and real-time data for lifecycle optimization. It aligns with the general definition provided in the introduction. Supported by quotes: 'virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The extraction accurately summarizes the high-level services mentioned in the abstract and introduction, such as real-time monitoring and predictive analytics. However, it misses specific technical services like interactive visualization and control configuration described in the detailed sections. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations' and 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction correctly identifies Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and Unreal Engine for visualization. However, it includes unsupported descriptors such as 'secure' and 'high-fidelity' which are not found in the provided source text, resulting in a slightly inflated detail level.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately reflects the source text's description of the framework. It correctly identifies BIM as the source of geometric models and the enrichment with BCS components (sensors, actuators, controllers) defined by engineers, as stated in Section III-A: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'. It correctly describes the use of Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) for automatic generation of artifacts ('leveraging MDE principles to extract structured representations... and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts' in Section III-A) and the real-time synchronization ('real-time data exchange' in the Abstract). The extraction is comprehensive and highly specific to the described system.

The characteristic is correctly derived from the text, identifying the role of the Building Control Engineer and the flow of control signals to the BCS. It captures the specific modeling methodology described in the paper. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineer... Models and configures devices, defines the control logic', 'ability to... feed control signals back to the Building Control System (BCS)', and 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'.

This aligns well with the text's description of how sensors are handled within the framework—specifically, their configuration and embedding into the BIM model by engineers. Supported by quotes: 'runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations... embedded into BIM models', 'Building Control Engineer... Models and configures devices', and 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment'.

The extraction precisely identifies the specific validation metric mentioned in the text (ODTE) and its purpose. Supported by quotes: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric', and 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately describes the lifecycle stages based on the source text. It mentions 'initial device modeling within the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers', which aligns with Table I and Section III-B describing the roles. It describes 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to structured representations' and 'automatic generation of deployable artifacts', supported by the text stating the framework 'leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'. It also includes 'real-time data synchronization' ('ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'), 'interactive visualization' ('Unreal Engine 5 provides a rich visualization environment for interactive monitoring and simulation'), and 'modular integration of custom services' ('framework's modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics').

The description correctly identifies the twinning process as using 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models', which matches the source: 'Our approach systematically extracts relevant asset data from a BIM model and generates runtime interfaces... leveraging MDE principles to extract structured representations'. It accurately states 'automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention', supported by 'automating the creation, configuration, and deployment of DTs' and 'minimal manual intervention'. The evolution description is also well-grounded: 'Evolution is managed through a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points', supported by 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms' and 'allows DTs to evolve with changing operational requirements'.

The characteristic accurately captures the decision support capabilities described in the text. 'Interactive visualization interfaces for real-time system monitoring and control' is supported by 'Unreal Engine 5 provides a rich visualization environment for interactive monitoring and simulation' and 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control'. 'Analytics capabilities delivered through custom services integrated via standardized extension points' is supported by 'standardized extension points... supports the integration of custom services and analytics'. The extraction is specific and grounded in the provided sources.

The extraction precisely captures the standardization aspects of the framework. It correctly references the 'model-driven transformation pipeline' used to 'standardize BIM semantics' and create a 'unified semantic foundation'. The claim regarding compatibility with 'AECO-specific standards such as BIM' is directly supported by the text's assertion that concepts must be designed for 'compatibility with existing, AECO-specific systems and standards such as BIM'. The mention of 'standardized extension points' for interoperability is also explicitly present in the abstract and contributions section.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
